target: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 robustness paragraph — tighten the new "~20× short" sentence the decider self-applied for ISS-1742 (commit ac0e54f) so the rounding matches the underlying arithmetic.
source_iss: ISS-1742 (closed; this is a wording-precision tightening, not a re-open)
status_summary: Decider self-applied a one-sentence patch to §4.2.7 H3/robustness paragraph closing the slow-orbit defender escape (curmudgeon ISS-1742 H1). The math (~7 sec totality at observed Moon angular speed of ~0.25°/min × dome-published h_Moon = 2,534 km) is reproduced and consistent with EXP-272 math_verification.angular_velocity field. However the prose says the totality figure is "still more than 20× short of the observed 138-second maximum" — the actual ratio is 19.3-19.6× (1.32 km diameter / (0.25°/min × 2534 km × π/180) × 60 = 7.16 sec; 138.2 / 7.16 = 19.3×). Curmudgeon's recommended phrasing was "~20× shortfall" / "still ~20× short" — the decider's "more than 20×" overshoots slightly. Tighten to match the arithmetic.
holes_addressed: Wording-precision drift: "more than 20× short" overstates the actual 19.3-19.6× ratio. Same class of imprecision we critique the dome model for (rounded figures presented as harder bounds than the math supports). The fix is a one-word substitution.

patch_specs.uniqueness_check: Substring "still more than 20× short of the observed 138-second maximum" appears exactly once in part4.html (verified 2026-04-30 by analyst node grep).
patch_specs.old_string: still more than 20× short of the observed 138-second maximum
patch_specs.new_string: still close to 20× short of the observed 138-second maximum
patch_specs.rationale: Strict math: t = 1.30 km / (0.25°/min × 2,534 km × π/180) × 60 = 7.06 sec → 138.2/7.06 = 19.6×. Using totality diameter 1.32 km (2 × R_u where R_u = 0.66 km from the §4.2.7 prose) gives 7.16 sec → 19.3×. "more than 20×" requires <6.91 sec, which would need either a smaller totality diameter than 1.3 km or a faster Moon angular speed than 0.25°/min — neither is consistent with the sentence's own anchoring. "close to 20×" preserves the rhetorical force (the slow-orbit escape route is closed by an order-of-magnitude shortfall) without overstating. Curmudgeon's original recommendation in SEC-4.2.7-EXP272.c1.json holes_found[0] explicitly said "~20× shortfall" / "still ~20× short" — this restores curmudgeon's phrasing.

math_verification.derivation_chain: ω_Moon ≈ 0.25°/min (apparent diurnal angular motion of the Moon across the sky for a fixed Earth observer; dominated by Earth's rotation 360°/24h = 0.25°/min, with Moon's own orbital motion subtracting ~0.0083°/min to give ~0.2417°/min — the 0.25°/min figure is a standard rounded approximation cited in EXP-272 math_verification.standard_moon_apparent_angular). At the dome-published h_Moon = 2,534 km, the corresponding linear velocity at zenith is v = ω·h = 0.25 × π/180 × 2534 = 11.057 km/min. Totality circle diameter = 2·R_u = 1.32 km (R_u = 0.66 km from §4.2.7 prose). Per-observer totality = diam/v × 60 = 1.32/11.057 × 60 = 7.165 sec, or 7.054 sec using the rounded 1.30 km diameter the prose itself uses earlier. Ratio vs observed 138.2 sec: 19.29× (at 1.32) or 19.59× (at 1.30).
math_verification.consistent_with_EXP272: YES. EXP-272.math_verification.angular_velocity recorded the dome moon's zenith angular speed as 1.97°/min (rim) and 0.566°/min (mid-disc), with the standard observed value at 0.25°/min noted in the standard_moon_apparent_angular field. The new sentence is the natural inverse: constraining the dome moon to match the observed 0.25°/min and asking what totality duration that implies. The two derivations use the same ω = v/h relation; only the input is reversed.
math_verification.rounding_diagnosis: For "more than 20×" to be strictly true at the published anchor (h_Moon = 2,534 km, totality diameter ≈ 1.30 km), the angular speed would need to be ≥ 0.255°/min — slightly above the 0.25°/min cited. Symmetrically, for ω = 0.25°/min the implied diameter would need to be ≤ 1.27 km. The decider's "more than 20×" is therefore overshooting by ~2-4% on either input. "close to 20×" or "still ~20× short" both fit the arithmetic without rhetorical loss.

cross_section_impact.p4_self_consistency: No conflict with the rest of §4.2.7. Existing ratios in the same paragraph: 1/220 width and 1/150 duration at 87 km/min rim-orbit; 1/45 duration at 25 km/min mid-disc. The new ~1/20 ratio at 11 km/min observed-Moon speed extends the robustness ladder without contradiction.
cross_section_impact.p4_4_2_7a_addendum: §4.2.7a (station coverage) does not reference the 0.25°/min figure or the 7-sec duration; it operates at the rim-orbit upper bound throughout. No update needed.
cross_section_impact.figures: eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg captions "sub-second spike" (rim-orbit case only) — unaffected. eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg bullet panel reports 0.9 sec / 1/150 ratio (rim-orbit) — also unaffected; ISS-1743 already tracks the optional follow-up of adding a slower-orbit bullet.
cross_section_impact.other_files: Searched data/sections.json (all parts), data/wins.json, data/predictions.json for "0.25", "~7 sec", "20×", "138-second". The only occurrence of 0.25°/min eclipse-related is the new sentence itself (part1 has an unrelated "0.25" sensitivity-analysis mention; wins.json has unrelated 0.25 mentions in WIN-005 analemma and a redshift cluster context). No cross-section references need updating.
cross_section_impact.test_coverage: No test in test.js asserts the "more than 20× short" wording. Patch is test-safe.

acceptance_criteria: P1 substring is unique in part4.html (single occurrence — verified)
After P1 applied: node test.js passes
After P1 applied: node build.js html regenerates docs/index.html with the corrected wording
No new issue should be opened for ISS-1742 — that closure stands; this is a precision tightening only
decider_instructions: 1. Apply P1 (substring_replace in data/sections.json part4.html). Confirm uniqueness via grep before substitution.
2. Run node test.js — should still pass; no test asserts the >20× wording.
3. Run node build.js html (or build.js publish if other patches are queued).
4. No issue to close — ISS-1742 is already closed; this is a wording-precision follow-up. Optionally add a one-line note in the EXP-272 / ISS-1742 closure record pointing at this EXP for audit trail.
5. Update tracker entry for EXP-281: status=integrated, integrated_at=<now>, integrated_by=decider.
kernel_of_truth: The decider's added sentence is well-targeted: it takes EXP-272's buried math_verification.standard_moon_apparent_angular observation and surfaces it in prose as a defender-escape closure — exactly what the curmudgeon recommended. The argument is correct on the merits. The kernel here is real: closing the slow-orbit escape with an order-of-magnitude shortfall is the right move. What the kernel does not save is the precise numerical claim — "more than 20×" overshoots by a few percent. We hold ourselves to the same numerical-precision standard we apply to the dome's 94.5%-accuracy figure.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 0.25°/min figure is itself a rounded approximation; the actual Moon apparent diurnal motion can range 0.24-0.255°/min depending on lunar phase and orbital geometry. At the upper end (0.255°/min) the ratio crosses 20× exactly. The decider is rounding consistently with the input precision.
anticipated_objections.response: Counter-counter: the prose anchors 0.25°/min, not a range. If the input were 0.255°/min the sentence would read "~0.255°/min." Once 0.25 is asserted, the math gives 19.6×, and "more than 20×" misrepresents that. If we want to use the upper-end angular speed, we should write "0.25 to 0.26°/min, giving a ~20× shortfall" — but then the wording change is even larger. Cleanest fix: keep 0.25 anchored, change "more than" to "close to."

anticipated_objections.objection: The fix weakens the rhetorical force of the closure. The defender can now argue we conceded the slow-orbit escape as "only" 20× shortfall instead of "more than" 20×.
anticipated_objections.response: A 20× shortfall is itself decisive — it's an order-of-magnitude failure, not a quibble. The argument is preserved: "close to 20× short" is still catastrophic for the dome. The decider already made the bigger rhetorical move (closing the escape route with an order-of-magnitude argument); this is just precision cleanup. We protect our credibility by being numerically honest, the same way we critique the dome for not being.

anticipated_objections.objection: This is too small to merit a fresh EXP — should be inlined into a future c2 cleanup pass.
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged the smallness. But ISS-1742 was JUST closed (2026-04-30T05:23Z), the curmudgeon explicitly recommended "~20×" wording, and the decider deviated to "more than 20×". Catching the drift on the same-day cycle keeps the audit trail clean. The EXP is ~one-line patch + math justification — minimal overhead. Alternative: skip EXP, let curmudgeon catch it on c2. But the curmudgeon already DID catch it (ISS-1742 H1 recommendation said "~20×"); the issue is the decider integration drift, which is best caught at the analyst-verification stage rather than waiting another c2 cycle.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not just change "more than" to "about" instead of "close to"?
anticipated_objections.response: "about 20×" works equally well. "close to 20×" matches a slightly more cautious register (the actual value is 19.3-19.6×, which is "close to" but slightly under 20). Either is fine; "close to" is the default suggestion and the decider can swap if a stylistic match to the surrounding prose is preferred.

exp_meta.rationale: Same-cycle integration-drift catch. Decider self-applied a curmudgeon-recommended patch but rounded the numerical claim slightly above the underlying arithmetic. One-line fix to bring prose back into agreement with the math — the analyst Mode 2b verification trigger explicitly asks for this kind of consistency check.
